runs.t: SQL (pronounced "S-Q-L" or "sequel") is the language used to communicate with a database. It lets you retrieve, add, update and delete records. In Delphi, SQL is added to a
Think of a database as a massive spreadsheet with thousands of rows. SQL is how you
: "Give me all students in Grade 12 with a mark above 80, sorted by surname." Without SQL, you would have to scroll through every row manually.
component. Always close the query before changing the SQL, then open it again.

blocks.label: Delphi — two ways to run a query
SQL
blocks.text:
SELECT * FROM tblCD;                      -- all fields, all records
SELECT CDName, Artist FROM tblCD;          -- only these two fields
SELECT DISTINCT Genre FROM tblCD;          -- each genre shown once only
SELECT TOP 5 * FROM tblCD;               -- first 5 records only

runs.t: clause is how you filter — like asking "show me only the records that match this condition."

blocks.label: SQL — comparison operators
blocks.text:
WHERE Genre = "Rock"             -- exact match (text in double quotes)
WHERE Price < 100               -- numbers (no quotes)
WHERE Grade <> 11              -- not equal
WHERE Mark >= 50              -- greater than or equal
WHERE Genre = "Pop" AND Price < 100       -- both must be true
WHERE Genre = "Pop" OR Genre = "Rock"     -- either can be true

rows.t: Match any value in a list

blocks.label: LIKE wildcard examples
blocks.text:
WHERE CDName LIKE "S%"        -- starts with S
WHERE CDName LIKE "%love%"    -- contains the word "love"
WHERE CDName LIKE "%s"        -- ends with s
WHERE Code   LIKE "A__"       -- starts with A followed by exactly 2 chars

blocks.label: SQL
blocks.text:
SELECT * FROM tblCD ORDER BY Artist;             -- ascending (default)
SELECT * FROM tblCD ORDER BY Price DESC;        -- most expensive first
SELECT * FROM tblCD ORDER BY Genre, CDName ASC; -- sort by genre, then name

runs.t: You can do maths inside a SELECT and give the result a name using

blocks.label: SQL
blocks.text:
SELECT Name, Price, Price * 1.15 AS PriceWithVAT FROM tblProducts;
SELECT Name, Mark, ROUND(Mark / 2, 0) AS HalfMark FROM tblStudents;

blocks.label: SQL — GROUP BY and HAVING
blocks.text:
-- Count students per grade
SELECT Grade, COUNT(*) AS Total
FROM tblStudents
GROUP BY Grade;

-- Only show groups with more than 10 students
SELECT Grade, COUNT(*) AS Total
FROM tblStudents
GROUP BY Grade
HAVING COUNT(*) > 10;

runs.t: GROUP BY. If you have both, WHERE runs first.

runs.t: In SA IT CAPS, joining two tables is done using the
— listing both tables in FROM and linking them with a matching condition in WHERE. This is the only join method required for CAPS.

blocks.title: CAPS Method: WHERE Clause — Not INNER JOIN

runs.t: The official SA IT CAPS specifies the
for single joins. The CAPS document states:
“Create a join query (single joins) using WHERE”
. Do NOT use the INNER JOIN keyword in exams.
A relational database splits related data across tables. For example, a student record stores a ClassID but the class name lives in a separate table. To display both together, you join the tables.

blocks.label: SQL — WHERE join syntax

runs.t: clause links the tables on their shared key field.

blocks.text: Example 1 — Students with their class names

blocks.label: SQL

blocks.text: Example 2 — With extra filtering and sorting

blocks.label: SQL
blocks.text:
SELECT tblStudents.Name, tblStudents.Surname, tblClasses.ClassName
FROM tblStudents, tblClasses
WHERE tblStudents.ClassID = tblClasses.ClassID
AND tblStudents.Grade = 12
ORDER BY tblStudents.Surname ASC;

runs.t: after the join condition to filter results further. The join condition always comes first in WHERE.

blocks.text: Example 3 — Join with GROUP BY and COUNT

blocks.label: SQL — count students per class
blocks.text:
SELECT tblClasses.ClassName, COUNT(tblStudents.StudentID) AS Total
FROM tblStudents, tblClasses
WHERE tblStudents.ClassID = tblClasses.ClassID
GROUP BY tblClasses.ClassName
ORDER BY Total DESC;
Example 4 — Using LIKE with a join

blocks.label: SQL
blocks.text:
SELECT tblBooks.Title, tblAuthors.AuthorName, tblBooks.Price
FROM tblBooks, tblAuthors
WHERE tblBooks.AuthorID = tblAuthors.AuthorID
AND tblBooks.Title LIKE "IT%"
ORDER BY tblBooks.Price DESC;

runs.t: If you list two tables in FROM but omit the WHERE link condition, SQL returns every possible combination of rows (a
). For two tables with 100 rows each, this produces 10,000 rows — all incorrect. Always include
OOP lets you model real-world things as
as a blueprint (like architectural plans for a house) and an
as the actual building made from that plan. One blueprint, many buildings — one class, many objects.

rows.t: A blueprint or template that describes what an object looks like and can do
A specific instance of a class (like one particular cat — Whiskers)
A variable that stores data about the object (e.g. fName, fAge)
A procedure or function that belongs to the class (e.g. GetName, SetAge)
that creates an instance and sets initial values
Hiding private data inside a class; only accessible through public methods
A new class inherits all attributes and methods of an existing (parent) class
The same method name behaves differently in different classes

runs.t: In Delphi, every component is an object.
is an object. OOP lets you create your own custom classes.
A class is defined once. You can create as many objects (instances) from it as you need — each with its own data.
Before coding a class, design it using a
diagram — a rectangle divided into 3 sections.
— hidden from the outside world. Only expose them through controlled
methods (getters and setters). This protects data from accidental or invalid changes.

blocks.label: Delphi — encapsulation
blocks.text:
private
  fName: string;         // hidden from outside — direct access blocked
public
  procedure SetName(s: string);  // controlled write access (can include validation)
  function  GetName: string;     // controlled read access

runs.t: A child class inherits all attributes and methods from a parent class, and can add or override its own. This avoids repeating code.

Returns the value of a private attribute. Read-only access.
Updates the value of a private attribute. Can include validation.
Returns a string representation of the object's state — used for display.
Internal helper methods used by other methods, not accessible from outside.

blocks.title: Owner vs Parent — what's the difference?

runs.t: the component from memory when the form closes.
the component is drawn. They are usually the same form, but not always (e.g. Owner = Form, Parent = a Panel on that form).
If you create a component with
when you are done, otherwise it stays in memory. Passing the form as the owner lets Delphi clean up for you automatically.
A two-dimensional array stores data in a grid of rows and columns — like a table or spreadsheet. Each element is accessed using two indices: [row, column].

is a single variable that stores many values arranged in a grid of rows and columns. Where an ordinary (one-dimensional) array is like a single shopping list running down the page, a 2D array is like a whole spreadsheet — a block of cells where every value sits at the crossing point of a row and a column.

think of a spreadsheet such as Excel. To find a cell you give a column letter and a row number — for example C5. A 2D array works exactly the same way, except we use two numbers: the first for the row and the second for the column. So
means "the value in row 2, column 3", just like clicking one cell in a grid.

that naturally have rows and columns — for example a class register of marks where each row is a learner and each column is a subject, a seating plan, a noughts-and-crosses board, or temperatures recorded for each day across several weeks. One neat variable holds the whole table instead of dozens of separate variables.
… you keep every mark in one organised grid.
– a row can represent one learner and a column one subject, so the position itself tells you what the value is.
– because the data is regular, two nested loops can fill, display or total the entire table in a few lines.
Before you can use a 2D array you must
it, telling Delphi how many rows and columns it has and what type of data each cell holds. You give two ranges in the square brackets: the first range is the rows and the second is the columns.

blocks.label: Syntax
blocks.text:
var
  arrName : array [rowStart..rowEnd, colStart..colEnd] of DataType;

runs.t: The example above creates a grid with 2 rows and 3 columns, giving 6 integer cells in total.
the ranges can start at any number, but in CAPS we almost always start at 1 so that "row 1" really is the first row.

The table below shows how the index numbers map onto the grid. Read it like a spreadsheet: pick a row down the side and a column across the top, and the cell where they meet is the array element you are referring to.
means putting values into the cells. There are two common ways: assigning each cell one by one (fine for a few known values), or using nested loops to fill the whole grid quickly.
Here we set each cell directly by naming its row and column. This is clear but tedious — imagine doing it for a 30 × 6 register. Use it only when you have a handful of specific values to place.

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
myArray[1,1] := 10;  myArray[1,2] := 20;  myArray[1,3] := 30;
myArray[2,1] := 40;  myArray[2,2] := 50;  myArray[2,3] := 60;

runs.t: This is the technique you will use most often. The
moves through the rows; for each row the

moves through every column. The result is that the inner loop runs completely for each turn of the outer loop, so the code visits every cell, one full row at a time — exactly like reading a spreadsheet left-to-right, then dropping down to the next line.

blocks.label: Delphi

runs.t: To show the grid neatly we again use nested loops, but this time we build up one line of text per row. For each row we start with an empty string, add every column value to it (padding each so the columns line up), and only then add the finished line to the memo.
the line is built inside the inner loop but written out in the outer loop — that is what keeps each row on its own line.

blocks.label: Delphi — display as table in memo

runs.t: A big reason for using a grid is to add things up. The trick is knowing which index to
. If you think of the class register again: a
is one learner's marks added across all subjects, while a
is one subject's marks added across all learners.
To total a single row we
(here row 1) and let the column index
change. The loop walks across that one row, adding each cell to a running total. This answers "what is everything in this row added together?" — for example one learner's total mark.

blocks.label: Delphi — total of row 1

runs.t: Now we do the opposite: we
(here column 2) and let the row index
change. The loop runs straight down that one column, adding each cell. This answers "what is everything in this column added together?" — for example the total of all learners' marks for one subject.

blocks.label: Delphi — total of column 2

runs.t: cell in the whole grid we need nested loops again — the outer loop for rows, the inner loop for columns — so the running total picks up each cell exactly once. This is how you would work out the grand total of all marks for the entire class across all subjects.

blocks.label: Delphi

runs.t: Recursion is when a function or procedure calls itself repeatedly until a base condition is met. It is a powerful technique for problems that can be broken down into smaller versions of the same problem.
is when a function calls itself to solve a smaller version of the same problem, again and again, until the problem becomes so small it can be answered directly.

picture a set of Russian nesting dolls. You open the biggest doll and find a smaller doll inside, open that and find a smaller one still, and you keep going until you reach the tiny doll that does not open. Recursion works the same way — each call opens up a slightly smaller version of the problem, and the smallest doll (the one that does not open) is the point where you stop.
routine is one that calls itself. Every recursive solution must have two parts:
— the condition that stops the recursion (essential — without it you get infinite recursion)
— the part where the function calls itself with a simpler input
A recursive function without a base case will call itself forever, causing a stack overflow error and crashing the program.
Factorial of n (written n!) = n × (n-1) × (n-2) × … × 1. Example: 5! = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120

blocks.label: Delphi — recursive factorial
Trace
blocks.text:
Factorial(4)
  = 4 * Factorial(3)
      = 3 * Factorial(2)
          = 2 * Factorial(1)
              = 1        ← base case reached
          = 2 * 1 = 2
      = 3 * 2 = 6
  = 4 * 6 = 24

blocks.label: Delphi — recursive sum

blocks.text: Example 3 — Power (x to the power of n)

blocks.label: Delphi

runs.t: Each number is the sum of the previous two: 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13…

blocks.label: Delphi

rows.t: Often more elegant for naturally recursive problems
Usually easier to trace and debug
Uses call stack (risk of stack overflow for large n)
Can be slower due to function call overhead

blocks.text: Key Rules for Writing Recursive Functions

runs.t: that returns a value without calling itself
Each recursive call must move toward the base case
(not procedure) so it can return a value
Modern databases are fed by many automated data collection methods. Large organisations store historical data in warehouses for analysis and decision-making.

is the process of gathering raw facts and figures from many different sources so that they can be stored, processed and turned into useful information. In the old days a person sat with a clipboard and wrote everything down by hand. Today most data is collected
— every time you swipe a card, drive under an e-toll gantry or tap a website, data is being captured about you without anyone writing a single thing.

A database is only as good as the data inside it. The more accurate, complete and up-to-date the collected data is, the better the decisions an organisation can make. Automated collection means more data, collected faster, with fewer human mistakes.

When you scan your Pick n Pay Smart Shopper card at the till, the shop instantly records what you bought, when, where and for how much — that single swipe feeds a database that the shop later uses to send you targeted specials.

rows.t: User types data directly into a form
Online interactive pages with GUI components (dropdowns, checkboxes)
Wireless chips store ID data, read without contact
Library books, inventory, e-toll, access cards
Small files stored by websites to record browsing behaviour and preferences
Login sessions, shopping carts, ad targeting
Records each purchase: date, time, location, product, amount
Collect app usage, location, device data
Uber location tracking, fitness app steps
Fixed, does not change once recorded
Address of a shop, GPS coordinates of a landmark
Changes as the object moves, updated continuously
Uber driver location, delivery truck tracking

runs.t: is a large central storage system that collects and consolidates
data from many separate source databases into one single place, so that it can be analysed for trends and decision-making.
Think of it like this: a normal database is like the till at one Pick n Pay branch — it records today's sales as they happen. A data warehouse is like head office in Cape Town, where the sales from

branch in the country, going back many years, are all poured into one giant store. Head office does not use this to ring up a sale; they use it to spot the big picture — which products sell best in winter, which branches are growing, where to open the next store.
– one single store that brings many sources together.
– it keeps years of old data, not just today's transactions.
– data is loaded in and then read for reports; it is not constantly changed.
– organised around topics like "sales" or "customers" for easy analysis.
Because a warehouse holds so much data from so many sources, the data must first be
and put into a consistent format before it is loaded in — otherwise the same customer might appear three different ways and the analysis would be wrong.

is the process of analysing very large datasets to discover hidden patterns, trends and useful relationships that a human could never spot by eye. The name is a good clue: just as a gold miner digs through tonnes of rock to find a few grams of gold, data mining digs through millions of records to find the valuable nuggets of knowledge buried inside.
Companies collect enormous amounts of data, but raw data on its own is useless — it is just numbers and words. Data mining turns that raw data into

they can act on. For example, Pick n Pay's Smart Shopper data might reveal that customers who buy nappies on a Friday evening also tend to buy snacks — so the shop places those products near each other and increases sales. Nobody told the computer to look for that link; the pattern was
— use SQL to pull only the required subset
: target campaigns based on customer age, interests, behaviour
: predict patient risk factors from historical data
: Facebook mines your likes to suggest friends and content

means protecting the accuracy, security and availability of an organisation's data throughout its life. Data is one of the most valuable assets a company owns — often worth more than its buildings or equipment — because decisions, money and reputations all depend on it. Poor data leads to poor decisions, and lost data can shut a business down completely.

"Garbage in, garbage out." If incorrect data goes into the system, only incorrect information can come out — no matter how clever your analysis is. This is why validation and verification at the point of capture are so important.
Below are the main ways an organisation looks after its data. Notice how each one protects against a different danger — wrong data, lost data, or stolen data.
— ensure entered data meets rules (format, range, type)
— ensure data is correct and intentional (double entry, confirmation)
— audit trail recording who changed what and when
— duplicate copies in separate locations for disaster recovery
A relational database stores data in structured tables linked together. Normalisation is the process of cleaning up a database design to remove repetition and prevent errors.
A relational database stores data in
(also called relations). Each table represents one type of thing — for example, a table for students, a separate table for subjects. Tables are then

rows.t: Uniquely identifies each record. No duplicates, no blanks.
A field that links to the primary key of another table. Creates the relationship.
Two or more fields combined to create a unique identifier
Could be a primary key but wasn't chosen as one
Email address (also unique, but ID was chosen as PK)

runs.t: Referential integrity is a rule that ensures relationships between tables remain consistent. It means:
a foreign key value must always match an existing primary key value
doesn't exist in tblClasses, you cannot add a student with ClassID =
. The database will reject this to prevent orphan records (records with no matching parent).
is a diagram that shows the
between them. Each box is a table; the line between boxes shows how they relate. You are often asked to
one in the exam, so practise the crow's-foot notation below.
The relationship is created by putting the
of the "one" table into the "many" table as a

rows.t: One record links to exactly one record in the other table
One record links to many records in the other table
One book can appear in many loans
Many records link to many records — resolved with a third "link" table

runs.t: A relational database can't store a many-to-many relationship directly. You break it into two one-to-many relationships using a

rows.t: The data is not lost or damaged by hardware failure, power loss or disaster
RAID, UPS, regular backups, off-site storage
The data stays correct, consistent and valid
Validation rules, referential integrity, record locking

runs.t: temporarily "locks" a record while one user is editing it, so that a second user cannot change the same record at the same time. This prevents two users overwriting each other's changes and keeps the data consistent (a key way to protect
When a database is poorly designed (not normalised), three types of errors can occur when working with data:

rows.t: You can't add new data without first adding other unrelated data
Can't add a new class until at least one student enrols in it
Deleting one record accidentally removes other important data
Deleting the only student in a class also deletes the class's information
Changing one value means updating many rows — and missing some creates inconsistency
A teacher's name appears in 50 student records; update one and the others are wrong

runs.t: Normalisation splits large, messy tables into smaller, focused tables and links them properly. This eliminates all three anomalies.
(no lists of values in one cell)
❌ Problem: Three values in one cell (Subjects column)
A table is in 2NF when it is in 1NF
every non-key field depends on the
primary key (not just part of it). This only matters when the primary key is a composite key.
❌ Problem: StudentName only depends on StudentID, not on (StudentID + SubjectID) together
A table is in 3NF when it is in 2NF
no non-key field depends on another non-key field (no transitive dependency).
❌ Problem: GradeDescription depends on GradeLevel (a non-key field), not on StudentID

rows.t: Multiple values in one cell? Repeating columns?
Split into separate rows; add PK
Non-key field depends on only PART of the composite PK?
Move that field to its own table
Non-key field depends on another non-key field?
Move the dependency to its own table
Data is accurate, consistent and complete throughout its lifecycle
Changing the database structure doesn't break the programs that use it
Minimum — the same data should not be stored in multiple places
Only authorised users can access or modify data
The database design makes it simple to insert, update and delete without causing problems

runs.t: Mobile device hardware, factors that affect computer performance, and choosing the right system for a user's needs.
Smartphones have similar hardware to desktops but in miniaturised form. Key differences:

rows.t: Separate chip; very powerful; many cores
SoC (System on a Chip) — CPU+GPU+modem+RAM in one chip
Limited battery; performance vs battery trade-off

runs.t: : limited capacity must last the whole day; processing power directly affects battery drain
: miniaturised components mean less cooling and tighter power limits
: limited display area affects usability for complex tasks
: touchscreen less precise than physical keyboard/mouse for complex input

rows.t: Higher = more instructions per second; faster processing
More cores = better multitasking and parallel processing
More RAM = more programs running simultaneously; higher speed = faster data transfer to CPU
Larger cache = CPU waits less for data from RAM
Higher GPU speed = faster graphics rendering; important for gaming and video
SSD dramatically faster boot times and application loading
Faster NIC = faster downloads, streaming, network file transfers
Outdated OS causes compatibility issues, security risks and slower operation

Processes vast amounts of data faster than humans
Reduces human error in repetitive tasks

No true understanding or common sense — only pattern matching

Software, storage and computing power delivered as a service over the Internet, rather than installed locally.

rows.t: Affordable — pay only for what you use

Cloud reduces the need for powerful local hardware — thin clients and low-spec devices can run complex software via the cloud. Businesses no longer need to buy expensive servers.
on a single physical computer. Each VM has its own OS, RAM, storage and apps, isolated from others.

: distributing Linux distros, large software updates

: access shared folders from another PC in the same building

rows.t: Converts readable data into unreadable code. Only someone with the correct key can decrypt it.
Protocol securing communication between browser and server (HTTPS). Uses public/private key pairs.
Issued by a trusted Certificate Authority. Verifies the website is legitimate.
Filters incoming and outgoing traffic based on security rules
Multi-Factor Authentication — requires password + second factor (OTP, fingerprint)
One-Time PIN — valid for one login only; prevents replay attacks

runs.t: A protocol that splits large files into pieces shared between many users simultaneously.
: very fast for large files, efficient bandwidth use
: often used for illegal (pirated) content; potential malware; your IP is visible
Legal uses: Linux distribution downloads, game updates, large software releases
Types of cybercrime, the people who commit them, their effects on individuals and organisations, and the safeguards used to prevent them.

rows.t: Gains unauthorized access to systems. White hat (ethical) vs Black hat (malicious).
Breaks software protection or encryption for illegal access or distribution
Uses pre-written tools without understanding them; less skilled
Current or former employee who misuses access privileges
Commits crime for financial gain (fraud, ransomware, identity theft)
Stealing personal information to impersonate someone (open accounts, get loans)
Fake emails that look official to steal credentials
Redirecting users to fake websites to steal information
Encrypts victim's data; demands payment to restore access
Stealing trade secrets, customer databases, or financial records
Inserting malicious SQL via web forms to manipulate or steal database data
Distributed Denial of Service — overwhelming a server with traffic to take it offline
Scams, fake auctions, advance-fee fraud (419 scams)
Harassment, threats or humiliation via digital channels
Illegally copying, distributing or using copyrighted software/media

runs.t: Financial loss — stolen money or fraudulent accounts opened in your name
Legal complications if identity is used for crimes
Financial loss from stolen funds, ransom payments, or system recovery
Loss of competitive advantage if trade secrets are stolen
Reputational damage and loss of customer trust
Legal penalties for failing to protect customer data
Operational downtime while systems are restored

rows.t: Scrambles data so intercepted data is unreadable
Makes accounts much harder to compromise
Allows recovery after ransomware or data loss
Fixes known vulnerabilities before criminals exploit them
Educated users are less likely to click phishing links
Least-privilege principle: users only access what they need
Prevents SQL injection by sanitising user input
Verifies website legitimacy; encrypts all data in transit

runs.t: In South Africa, cybercrime is covered by:
: criminalises hacking, ransomware, online fraud, malicious communications
: governs how personal data must be collected, stored and protected
How Big Data, data warehousing, globalisation and the 4IR affect society — privacy, employment, social media and the evolution of the web.

blocks.text: Social Implications of Big Data & Data Mining

runs.t: is the term for datasets so enormous, fast-moving and varied that ordinary database tools cannot store or process them — think of every Facebook post, every WhatsApp message and every e-toll swipe in the country, all at once. When companies and governments
this Big Data, the results affect real people's lives, and that brings both wonderful benefits and serious dangers.

The same data that lets a hospital predict and prevent illness can also let an insurance company refuse you cover because your shopping habits suggest you eat unhealthily. The technology is neutral — it is
the data is used that creates the social problem. As IT students you must be able to argue both sides.

rows.t: Privacy erosion — habits tracked without full consent
Security breaches expose millions of records
Data sold to third parties without user knowledge
World interconnected economically and culturally; faster trade; outsourcing; cultural exchange; increased competition
AI, machine learning, IoT, robotics, automation, biotechnology, AR/VR, Big Data
Routine jobs replaced by machines; new careers in data science, AI, cybersecurity
Must reskill constantly; digital literacy essential

runs.t: Key factors affecting computer performance and maintenance:
: outdated software causes security risks and slower performance
: insufficient space causes performance issues (virtual memory relies on it)
: use SMART scan / Disk Check to detect and fix errors

blocks.text: Evolution of Social Media and its Effects

runs.t: : constant exposure to content makes it hard to focus or verify facts
: users voluntarily share location, habits, preferences
: attackers use social platforms to manipulate users into revealing information
: collaborative knowledge (e.g. Wikipedia) — useful but reliability varies
: global connectivity promotes exchange but can dilute local traditions

rows.t: Track browsing behaviour; can be used for targeted advertising
Enables free expression; also enables cyberbullying and illegal activity
Global Unique Identifiers track behaviour across websites and apps
Apps continuously track movement; risk of stalking and profiling
Piracy is illegal and harms creators; but sharing also enables legal distribution

runs.t: Use strong, unique passwords for every account
Avoid posting sensitive personal information publicly
Verify links before clicking — beware of phishing
Only connect to trusted Wi-Fi networks
Periodically search your own name to see your digital footprint
Based on the 2026 IT CAPS. Grade 12 introduces SQL (Term 1), OOP & 2D arrays (Term 2). Paper 2 is cumulative across all Grades 10–12.
(3 hrs): Delphi programming — SQL, OOP, 2D arrays, recursion, file handling, arrays, general programming.

blocks.title: Term 1 • January – March
blocks.subtitle: SQL (all statements + WHERE join); data management & database design theory

cols.title: 🔬 Practical (Paper 1)
items: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, ORDER BY, DISTINCT
LIKE, IN, BETWEEN, IS NULL, wildcards, dates (#)
Aggregate: SUM, MIN, MAX, AVG, COUNT, TOP
SQL functions: ROUND, INT, LENGTH, LEFT, RIGHT, MID, FORMAT
Date functions: DAY, MONTH, YEAR, DATE
All SQL via TADOQuery in Delphi

cols.title: 📘 Theory (Paper 2)
items: Data collection: forms, RFID, cookies, transactions, loyalty cards
Caring for data: validation, verification, integrity, access control
Big Data, warehousing & mining: social implications

cols.title: 🔬 Practical (Paper 1)
items: Row totals, column totals, element access

cols.title: 📘 Theory (Paper 2)
items: Computer management: housekeeping, integrity, system protection
Cloud computing: SaaS, advantages, hardware effects
AR & Mixed Reality: uses, hardware, limitations
Computer criminals: hackers, crackers, cyber gangs
Types of cybercrime: identity theft, ransomware, phishing, botnets

blocks.title: Term 3 • July – September (prelim + PAT due)
blocks.subtitle: Consolidation; internet services, networks, communication security & social implications

cols.title: 📘 Theory (Paper 2)
items: Online apps: cookies, PHP/JS/XML, CSS, AJAX
SSL, public/private keys, digital certificates, firewall
Review Gr11 data security (blockchain, encryption, MFA)
Privacy: cookies, anonymity, GUIDs, file sharing

blocks.title: Term 4 • September – November — Final Exams
blocks.subtitle: No new content — case studies, exam revision

cols.title: 🔬 Paper 1 High-Priority Areas
items: Q3: OOP with multiple method types

cols.title: 📘 Paper 2 High-Priority Areas
items: Check Exam Extras for out-of-CAPS questions

runs.t: These topics are not explicitly required by CAPS but have appeared in NSC past papers (2015–2024). Knowing them can mean the difference between a pass and a distinction.
These Delphi functions, components and techniques are not listed in CAPS but appear regularly in exam questions or are needed to solve exam problems efficiently.

desc.t: Replaces all or first occurrence of a substring within a string.
s := StringReplace(s, 'old', 'new', [rfReplaceAll]);
A powerful list of strings — very useful for loading file lines into memory.
sl := TStringList.Create; sl.LoadFromFile('data.txt'); sl.Free;
Formats numbers/strings with precise control —
Format('%.2f', [rVal])
gives 2 decimal places. More powerful than FloatToStrF.
Generate random integers. Always call
Randomize;
first to seed the generator.
Random(100)
returns 0–99.
Let users browse for files at runtime.
if OpenDialog1.Execute then sFile := OpenDialog1.FileName;
Convert between TDate values and string representation. Used with TDateTimePicker.
sDate := DateToStr(dtpDate.Date);
Catch runtime errors gracefully — e.g. invalid StrToInt input.
try iNum := StrToInt(edtNum.Text); except ShowMessage('Invalid'); end;
Returns the number of lines in a Memo. Often used in loops to process all memo lines.
for i := 0 to Memo1.Lines.Count - 1 do
Case-insensitive string comparison — avoids problems when user types 'yes', 'YES' or 'Yes'.
if SameText(sInput, 'yes') then
Convert integers to hexadecimal strings and back. Sometimes tested in conjunction with number systems theory.
sHex := IntToHex(255, 4); // = '00FF'
Built-in boolean checks — cleaner than MOD 2.
if Odd(iNum) then // true if iNum is odd
Break
exits the loop immediately.
Continue
skips to the next iteration. Both appear in exam solutions — know the difference.

runs.t: These theory topics have appeared in NSC Theory papers but sit at the edge of or outside the formal CAPS scope. They typically appear in social implications or "current technology" questions.

desc.t: AI appears regularly in social implications questions — automation of jobs, AI decision-making, bias in AI, chatbots. Not deeply technical — understand the concept and its social effects.
A distributed digital ledger of linked blocks. Used in cryptocurrency, supply chain, and secure records. Appeared in Gr12 theory papers. Know: what it is, how it works, and 2 uses.
AI-generated fake videos or audio that appear authentic. Social implications question topic — impacts on trust, truth, and politics. Know the definition and two risks.
A part of the internet not indexed by search engines, accessed via special software (Tor). Used for anonymity — legal and illegal uses. Appeared in theory cybercrime questions.
The principle that all internet traffic should be treated equally — ISPs cannot throttle or prioritise certain content. A social/political IT topic.
The successor to IPv4 — uses 128-bit addresses (vs 32-bit) to solve address exhaustion. Format: 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334. Know why it was introduced.
RAID 0: speed (striping, no redundancy). RAID 1: mirroring (exact copy). RAID 5: striping with parity (balance of speed + fault tolerance). CAPS only mentions RAID in general — exams go deeper.

Hiding secret information within ordinary files (images, audio) without detection. Different from encryption — encryption scrambles data; steganography hides its existence. Appeared in theory questions.
Self-executing contracts where terms are written in code on a blockchain. No middlemen needed. Related to blockchain technology — appeared in Gr12 social implications.
Processing data close to where it is generated (the "edge" of the network) rather than sending it all to a central cloud server. Reduces latency — important for IoT and 5G.

All devices share a single communication line (the bus). Not in the current CAPS but appeared in older papers. Advantage: cheap. Disadvantage: single point of failure; performance drops with more devices.
Manipulating people (rather than systems) to reveal confidential information. Includes pretexting, baiting, tailgating. Appears in cybercrime theory questions.
Exams frequently ask you to
convert
values — not just explain systems. Practise: decimal↔binary, decimal↔hex, binary↔hex. Know the place values (powers of 2 and 16).
South African law governing how personal data must be collected, stored, and protected. Appears in privacy and legal implications questions. Know: what it is, who it protects, and 2 requirements.
SA legislation that criminalises hacking, ransomware, online fraud, and malicious communications. Often referenced in cybercrime theory questions for Gr12.
A virtual replica of a physical object or system, updated in real time from sensor data. Used in engineering, manufacturing, and smart cities. Appeared in 4IR/IoT questions.

runs.t: A comprehensive reference of all Delphi functions you need to know — both CAPS-required and commonly appearing in exams.

rows.t: Position of substring (0 if not found)
Insert text into string at position
base to the power of exp
String → Integer (safe — returns default if invalid)
Off-by-one errors in loops; forgetting to initialise variables
Using wrong index (0 vs 1-based); not closing file
Class with constructor, accessors, mutators, toString (~25–30 marks)
Calling Create on the object var (not class name); forgetting Result in functions
TADOQuery, SQL statements including JOIN (~20–25 marks)
Not calling qry.Close before changing SQL; wrong JOIN ON condition
Write a recursive function; trace it (~10–15 marks)
Missing base case; not returning Result
Correct normal form identification; proper primary/foreign key use
Balance of positive AND negative effects; specific examples
Correct crime type names; effects on both victim and business

runs.t: Read the question twice — "list" means bullet points, "explain" means a sentence or two
Never leave a practical question blank — partial marks are awarded for correct structure
In SQL: always check your WHERE condition matches the question's criteria exactly
In OOP: write the constructor first — it sets up all the attributes you'll use elsewhere
In theory: "advantage AND disadvantage" means you need both for full marks
